coredata.dc:title: Development of highly selective novel fluorescence quenching probe based on Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticles for sensing the Fe(III)
coredata.dc:creator: Syal, Abhinandan
Sud, Dhiraj
coredata.dc:description: In the present paper, hetrostructured Bi2S3-TiO2 (BST) nanoparticle (NPs) was synthesized by modified hydrothermal technique using titanium butoxide as precursor of TiO2 and Bi(NO3)3 and thiosemicarbazide as precursors of Bi2S3. The synthesized BST NPs was characterized by using XRD, TEM and FTIR spectra. BST NPs form stable dispersion and displays high fluorescence emission at 818 nm. The synthesized nanoparticles show excellent fluorescent property and highly selective for Fe (III) ions due to binding affinity of the BST NPs with Fe (III) ion as evident from the higher value of Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv = 7859 M−1). The number of binding sites of BST NPs was evaluated from the double logarithm regression method and was found to be 0.948. The intensity of the fluorescence emission drop linearly in concentration range from 15 μM to 5000 μM and linear plot of the Stern-Volmer confirmed the static quenching of BST. The optimum pH obtained was 3.35. The detection limit of the synthesis BST sensor was 0.1173 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Fluorescence quenching
Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticles
Stern-Volmer equation
Ferric ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of interference of metal ions
Fluorescence quenching ability of metal ions viz monovalent, bivalent and trivalent
Absorption characteristics of BST with Fe3+
Influence of concentration of Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity of BST
Fluorescence spectra of BST at different pH
Interference of the other metal ions on florescence response of BST/Fe3+ system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The formation of the complex between fluorophore (BST NPs) and Fe(III).
The purposed energy transfer non-radiative quenching mechanisum path way between fluorophore (Fl) and Fe(III).

TEM images of Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticle (3a) size distribution histograms of nanotubes (3b) nanoparticles and (3c) lattice fringe with lattice disorder (marked in cercles). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence emission spectra of Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticle.
Relative fluorescence quenching of BST by different type of monovalent, bivalent and trivalent metal ions.
UV–vis spectra of BST (100 ppm) with vary the concentration of ferric ion from 800 μM to 30 μM.
Fluorescence titration of BST NPs with addition of different concentration of Fe3+ in aqueous solution (0 μM − 0.01 M).
Fluorescence performance of BST NPs at different pH range (1pH–11 pH).

a) Stern-Volmer plots I0/I versus [Fe3+] of the quenching of BST NPs (100 ppm) by Fe3+ (concentration range from 30 μM to 5000 μM). b) Double logarithm regression plot between log[(Io − I)/I] versus log [C].
Fluorescence response of BST NPs (100 ppm) in the presence of Fe3+ (600 μM) and/or various metal ions in aqueous media.
Compare the analytical characteristics of the determination of Fe3+ using BST NPs and mode of synthesis of fluorophore with earlier publications.

head.ce:title: Development of highly selective novel fluorescence quenching probe based on Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticles for sensing the Fe(III)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sant Longowal Institute of Engg. & Tech., Longowal, Sangrur, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sant Longowal Institute of Engg. & Tech.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present paper, hetrostructured Bi2S3-TiO2 (BST) nanoparticle (NPs) was synthesized by modified hydrothermal technique using titanium butoxide as precursor of TiO2 and Bi(NO3)3 and thiosemicarbazide as precursors of Bi2S3. The synthesized BST NPs was characterized by using XRD, TEM and FTIR spectra. BST NPs form stable dispersion and displays high fluorescence emission at 818 nm. The synthesized nanoparticles show excellent fluorescent property and highly selective for Fe (III) ions due to binding affinity of the BST NPs with Fe (III) ion as evident from the higher value of Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv = 7859 M−1). The number of binding sites of BST NPs was evaluated from the double logarithm regression method and was found to be 0.948. The intensity of the fluorescence emission drop linearly in concentration range from 15 μM to 5000 μM and linear plot of the Stern-Volmer confirmed the static quenching of BST. The optimum pH obtained was 3.35. The detection limit of the synthesis BST sensor was 0.1173 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Many transition metal ions (like Fe, Mn, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Mo etc.) are essential for living organisms. Among these metals, iron is most essential trace heavy element vital for many physiological processes and plays dominant role. The versatility of iron extends from its role as structural component of life molecule, haemoglobin to iron storage proteins, myoglobin, cytochromes as electron carrier and also part of enzyme catalysis for many metabolic reactions. The extensive repertoire of iron involvement in biological processes include oxygen transportation, transcriptional regulation, oxygen metabolism and electron transfer [1,2]. It also helps to prevent diseases such as Pancreas, heart, Alzhemier, Penkinson and liver [3–5]. The low solubility of hydrated Fe(III) (approximately 10−18 M) at physiological conditions emphasizes the difficulty of acquiring sufficient iron. On the other hand, excess of Fe (III) intake leads to adverse effects such as tissue damage due to the formation of free radicals. Therefore, iron concentrations in living organisms must be tightly regulated to control the disorders of iron metabolism leading to broad spectrum of diseases from anaemia to iron overload. Furthermore, the enormous variation in concentration of iron in different sources of water was observed and in particular the concentration of free ferric ion is extraordinarily low at neutral pH whatever may be the source of water. The maximum permitted limit of Fe (III) in drinking water is 5 μmol L−1 [6]. Therefore, the monitoring of iron levels has been an active issue in environmental analysis. But the lower concentration of Fe (III) ion limits its sensitivity and selectivity for practical analysis. Different methods have been adopted for the sensing of Fe (III) ions such as colorimetric analysis [7], mass spectroscopy [8], electrochemical [9,10], amperometeric [11,12] and florescence spectroscopy analysis [13–19]. Many research findings were devoted to the sensing of metal ions and other pollutants [27,28,44–53]. Megan et al. [20] differentiated Fe (II) and Fe (III) ion using fluorescence conjugated polymers as chemosensors. Raji et al. [21] synthesized nitrogen doped carbon dots for the detection of Fe (III) ion in water. Hani et al. [22] reported the fluorimetric sensing of the ferric ion by water soluble lanthanide metal organic framework. Jingshuai et al. [23] detected Fe (III) ion by the combination of fluorescent Fe3O4 −ZnO with l-cysteine. Natalia et al. [24] designed a coumarin- based turn-off fluorescent probe selective for ferrous ion. Jia et al. [25] synthesized photoluminescent nitrogen doped phenol formaldehyde resin polymer for detection of Fe (III) ion. Sharma et al. [26] synthesized CdS nanosheets for sensing of picric acid. Wang et al. [54] achieved the high selectivity and sensitivity for sensing Fe(III) using fluorescent organic nanoparticles. Yang et al. [55] slectively sense the Fe(III) ion by using fluorescence organic material.

The finding of these literature studies confirmed that fluorimetric spectroscopic technique is a favorite method due to its potential to offers higher sensitivity and selectivity. But complicated synthetic route needed for fluorescence organic dyes, their poor photo stability and toxicity of quantum dots in biological system are the major issue of concern. Keeping in mind these, the presentatier study reports the simple ultrasonicated assisted hydrothermal synthesis route of metal oxide showing highly sensitivity and selectivity. Further, to the best of our knowledge, most of the reported studies of sensing Fe (III) ion are restricted to dyes or biomolecules based fluorescence, quantum dots, chelate based sensor, etc. Sensing of Fe (III) by heterostructured Bi2S3 −TiO2 nanoparticles has not been reported yet. The sensitivity and selectivity of ferric ion are examined by varying the concentration of ferric ion and in the presence of other metal ions respectively.

Titanium Butoxide, Bismuth Nitrate, Thiosemicarbazide, CdCl2, CuCl2, SnCl2, PdCl2, NH4Cl, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3, HgCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, NiCl2, HCl, MnCl2 and FeCl2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ethanol and Isopropanol were used by the analytical grade.

TiO2 was synthesized as per reported in our previous paper [38]. Titanium (IV) n-butoxide was magnetic stirred in 20 ml of ethanol for two hours. After the addition of NH4OH, the jelly white precipitate was formed. Which were further stirred for two hours. The obtained precipitates were filtered by vacuum filtration, crystallized and dried in oven at 100 °C for two hours to get yellow color product.

Bismuth nitrate and thiosemicarbazide (mole ratio 1:3) were mixed with isopropyl alcohol: ethanol (1:1) solvent mixture and then was ultrasonicated for 40 min. The obtained dark brown precipitate of Bi2S3 was washed many times with ethanol: water (1:1) to remove impurities. The precipitate was dried for 2 h at 80 °C.

TiO2 and Bi2S3 synthesized in the first two steps were mixed together in a mole ratio of 1:7 respectively, followed by ultrasonicated for 10 min. The resultantant mixture was subjected to autoclave for 10 h under 12 psi pressure at 100 °C. The obtained precipitates of Bi2S3-TiO2 was washed with ethanol: water (1:1) mixture and dried for two hours at 80 °C.

An X-ray diffractometer (XPERT-PRO) employing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) was used for phase studies at the scanning rate of 5 min−1 in the 2θ angle range 20°–80°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was recorded on Bruker Tensor27 in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 by using KBr pellets at room temperature. The morphology and size of the nanoparticles were measured using transmission electron microscope (JSM-6610-JEOL/EO) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. The fluorescence spectra of BST solution recorded on Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength 540 nm and slit width of excitation and emission were kept at 3 nm and 5 nm respectively. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of interference of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of the synthesized material for Fe3+ ion, Interference studies were performed by the mixing of the solutions of other metal ions such as Cd2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Pd2+, NH4 +, Na+, K+ Fe2+ and Mn2+ at a constant concentration (600 μM) in Ferric ion solution. Specific amount of different solutions (ferric ion, other metal ion and BST) were mixed and ultrasonicated for 10 s, then fluorescence spectra of these solutions were recorded at excitation and emission wavelength 540 nm and 843 nm respectively.
The synthesized heterostructured Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticles were characterized using physio-chemical changes.

In XRD pattern, the sharp peaks of BST NPs indicated the complete crystallization of the BTS (Fig. 1 ). The most intense peaks, at 2θ = 25.55° corresponds to the (101) plane, which characteristic of the anatase phase with tetragonal crystalline structure of TiO2 [JCPDS 84–1285]. The peaks appeared at 2θ = 22.5°, 23.8°, 28.5°, 31.8° and 52.8 °corresponding to the orthorhombic crystalline structure of Bi2S3 of the planes, (220), (130), (121), (221) and (132), respectively [JCPDS 43-1471]. Hence XRD analysis confirmed the formation of anatase phase of TiO2 along with orthorhombic Bi2S3. The crystallite size (D) of BTS sample was estimated from the half width (β) of the peak at 2θ = 25.4° using the Scherrer formula:
D = kλ/(β cosθ)
where θ is the Bragg angle, and k is the Scherrer slope factor (0.96). The average crystalline size of BST was found to be 14 nm, which hold good with TEM analysis.

The synthesized BST NPs was examined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). The observed FTIR spectrum shows numerous well defined peaks in the spectra. The broad peak appearing 3450 cm−1 is related to the characteristic absorption peak of surface OH bonds. The appearance of peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 were due to the bending vibrations of HOH bond and stretching vibration of K-Br bond [29]. The peaks originated at 687 cm−1 were the characteristic stretching band vibrations of Bi–S bond.

TEM images of Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticles (BST) are shown in Fig. 3 a, which revealed a spherical morphology with narrow size distributions along with the nanotube. The size of the BST NPs was in range of 8–17 nm (Fig. 3b), Lattice fringes can be clearly seen in Fig. 3c. TEM images of BST nanoparticles exhibits a lattice fringe of about 0.31 nm, corresponding to the spacing of (211) crystal plane of orthorhromic Bi2S3 (Fig. 3c) (JCPDS 170320). Lattice disorder was noticed which was marked as red circles confirmed the heterostructured nature of synthesized nanoparticles (Fig. 3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching ability of metal ions viz monovalent, bivalent and trivalent
ce:section.ce:para: The possibility of fluorescence quenching by a transition metal center is an energy transfer process known as electronic energy transfer [42]. The metal center Fe(III) possessed half-filled energy levels, whose energy must be intermediate between the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of fluorophore [43]. This situation was achieved by doping of Bi2S3 with TiO2 resulting modified the band gap. Thus, double electron exchange takes place which restores the fluorophore (Fl) in its ground state and produced a metal center non-radiative d-d decay, by which fluorescence is quenched (Scheme 2). The double electron transfer occurs between BST/Fe(III) system was confirmed the strong interaction between Fl* and Fe (III), which further supported by our results obtained from UV–vis spectroscopy and higher value of a Stern-Volmer constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption characteristics of BST with Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–visible absorption spectroscopy is a simple technique to explore the structural changes occur in molecules and to recognize the complex formation between different chemical entities [30]. Hence the absorption spectra of BST in the presence of Fe3+ ion in the concentration range of 30–800 μM were recorded and shown in Fig. 6 . The absorption of BST NPs was observed around 238 nm. From UV-Spectral analysis decrease in intensity of the peak was noticed with a decrease in the concentration of Fe3+ ion and was also accompanied with a slight blue shift in the peak location at 238 nm. This can be explained on the basis of the interaction between BST nanoparticles and Fe3+ ion through a ground state complex formation, which is caused by the partial adsorption of Fe3+ ion on the surface of BST nanoparticles [30,31]. The equilibrium for the formation of the complex as purposed in following Scheme 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of concentration of Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity of BST
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of concentration of Fe3+ ion on the fluorescence behavior of BST was observed different concentration of Fe3+ ion (0.01 M–0 μM) (Fig. 7 ). The complete quenching of fluorescence intensity was observed beyond 0.01 M of Fe3+ concentration. The maxima of BST were gradual decrease with increase in ferric concentration, which can be exploited for the fluorimeteric based determination of Fe3+ ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of BST at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: Florescence properties show strongly dependent upon the pH of the solution. Therefore the effect of pH on the fluorescence response of BST was studied by varying the pH from 2 to 12 taking the concentration of both BST and Fe3+ ion constant (50 ppm and 200 μM respectively). The increase in fluorescence intensity was observed from 2 to 3.35 and thereafter decrease in value occurred (Fig. 8 ). Therefore, optimum pH selected was 3.35.

Stern-Volmer equation was applied to understand the interaction of quencher and fluorophore molecule. The Stern-Volmer equation in simplified form can be expressed as [39,40]
Io/I = 1 + KSV[C]
where Io and I are the fluorescence intensities observed in the absence and presence of quencher respectively. [C] is the concentration of quencher and KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of fluorophore can either be static or dynamic in nature depending upon molecular interactions involved in ground state or excited state process such as energy transfer, molecular rearrangement. The linear graph between (Io/I) versus concentration of Fe3+ (10–5000 μM) was attained with regression coefficient R2 value 0.9972. The higher value of Stern–Volmer constant (KSV) 7859 M−1 obtained from slope (Fig. 9 a), confirmed the strong interactions between Fe3+ and BST NPs. The fluorescence quenching of BST NPs involves the static quenching, the binding constant and the number of bindings sites can be evaluated from the double logarithm regression curve of log [(Io-I/I)] versus log [C], (Fig. 9b) based on the following equation [41]
log[(Io-I/I)] = logK + nlog[C]
where K is binding constant and [C] is the concentration of quencher. The results showed the number of binding sites for BST NPs per Fe3+ ion is 0.948 and the calculate value of ‘K’ is 846.64 M−1. The higher value of ‘K’ and ‘n’ revealed the effective interaction of BST NPs with Fe3+ ion which supports the formation of ground state complexation [37]. The detection limit, calculated from the 3σ IUPAC criteria was found to be 0.1173 μM, which is much lower than other previously reported values as shown in Table 1 [32–36]. Moreover, the method of synthesis of flurophore is simple and low cost as compare to reported studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of the other metal ions on florescence response of BST/Fe3+ system

ce:section.ce:para: The hetrostructured Bi2S3-TiO2 nanoparticles was synthesized by simple ultrasound assisted-hydrothermal method and characterized by XRD, FTIR and TEM analysis. TEM analysis confirmed the formation of nanotubes with dimensions of 215 nm × 9.60 nm along with spherical globules of 14 nm size. The XRD analysis represented the formation of two main phases, orthorhombic Bi2S3 and anatase TiO2. We have investigated the nature of interaction of BST NPs with eighteen metal ions including Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching method at optimum pH 3.35. The Stern-Volmer plot indicated the static quenching for BST/Fe3+ system. The number of binding sites and binding constant were found to be 0.948 and 846.64 M−1 respectively. The investigation of BST NPs showed higher selectivity, short response time (10 s) and lower detection limit (0.1173 μM) for Fe3+ ion. The presence of other metal ions having little influence on the selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ ion. Thus BST NPs can be employed as reliable and efficient fluorescence sensor for detection of Fe3+ ion with high selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the authorities of Sant Longowal Institute of Engineering and Technology for support. They wish to acknowledge the facilities provided by the SAIF, Cochin and Punjab University, Chandigarh.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Abhinandan Syal post graduated from Punjab University, Chandigarh for organic chemistry in 2010. He is currently working on his Ph.D. degree from Sant Longowal Institute of Engg. & Tech., Deemed University, Longowal, Sangrur, India. His current researches focus on nanomaterials and sensors.

Dhiraj Sud is a Professor in Sant Longowal Institute of Engg. & Tech., Deemed University, Longowal, Sangrur, India. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Punjab Agriculture University, Ludhiana, India in 1991. Her research interests involve nanomaterials, Adsorption of pollutant, photocatalytic degradation and sensors.
